behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: This behavior explicitly requires an external HTTP error contract check (401) plus a no-side-effect guarantee across boundaries (not forwarded). That matches SYSTEM TRIAD #2 (real external error-path response shape/contract). Unit tests of signature logic can pass while real handler wiring or middleware ordering still fails.
behaviors.test_description: Send a real HTTP webhook request with an invalid `X-Signature-256` to a running service instance; assert `401` response, then verify via queue readback/inspection that no event was persisted or enqueued.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This is an ordering guarantee across an external response boundary and persistent side effect, requiring round-trip observation (SYSTEM TRIAD #3). Mocked queue writes can hide real commit/flush timing issues.
behaviors.test_description: Issue a real HTTP request, capture response timing, and independently read the persistent queue state at response-return moment (or via atomic instrumentation) to prove the event is durably present before `200` is observed by the client.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: The behavior is about externally observable HTTP response timing versus asynchronous downstream completion, which depends on real runtime scheduling and response boundary behavior. Unit/integration stubs can miss event-loop/backpressure effects.
behaviors.test_description: Run service with intentionally slow downstream processor; send a real HTTP event and assert the response returns quickly while downstream processing completion occurs later (verified by delayed completion marker).

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: This is a wall-clock SLO under concurrency with externally visible HTTP timing, requiring real runtime/OS scheduling and queue contention behavior. In-process fakes frequently hide latency and contention failures.
behaviors.test_description: Generate concurrent load with earlier slow events in flight, then send a new webhook over real HTTP and assert end-to-end client-observed latency is <3s under defined test conditions.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: This is primarily a cross-component handoff/state-propagation case: "ID was already received" -> dedupe state must prevent second "written to the queue". It needs modules wired with real local persistence semantics, but no explicit SYSTEM TRIAD trigger is required in the behavior text. Rejected higher level: no explicit requirement for real external HTTP contract or OS/network/subprocess.
behaviors.test_description: With real dedupe store + queue modules wired together (local DB/filesystem allowed), submit the same event ID twice through handler/service boundary and assert only one queue record exists and second write path is skipped.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: This explicitly requires external HTTP error-path contract validation (status + header), matching SYSTEM TRIAD #2. Pure module-level tests can miss header/status mapping defects in the real server stack.
behaviors.test_description: Drive queue to full condition, send webhook via real HTTP, and assert response is `503` and includes correctly formatted `Retry-After` header.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: This is about queue + persistence module interaction and state enforcement under configured limits. It requires real local storage semantics but not explicit external HTTP contract checks, real network, subprocess, or LLM dependence. Rejected higher level: behavior text does not require externally visible API contract verification; it focuses on internal queue enforcement.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate queue with real persistence backend and configured capacity N; enqueue N events successfully, then assert enqueue N+1 is rejected with the queue’s defined local error/result.
